questions.question_text: The frequency of {tex}\gamma{/tex} -rays, X-rays and ultraviolet rays are a, b and c respectively. Then,
questions.question_html: The frequency of {tex}\gamma{/tex}-rays, X-rays and ultraviolet rays are a, b and c respectively. Then,
questions.explanation: As the wavelength of {tex}\gamma{/tex} -rays is least and that of ultraviolet rays is maximum, so the frequency of {tex}\gamma{/tex} -rays is maximum and that of ultraviolet rays is least.

questions.question_text: Maxwell’s displacement current is a response to inconsistency in the
questions.question_html: Maxwell’s displacement current is a response to inconsistency in the
questions.explanation: Maxwell showed that Ampere's circuital law is logically inconsistent. He considered a parallel plate capacitor being charged by a battery. He showed inconsistency of the law in the regions outside the plates of capacitor and just between the plates. To resolve this inconsistency displacement current was introduced by Maxwell.

questions.question_text: Without the concept of displacement current it is not possible to correctly apply Ampere’s law on a path parallel to the plates of parallel plate capacitor having capacitance C in
questions.question_html: Without the concept of displacement current it is not possible to correctly apply Ampere’s law on a path parallel to the plates of parallel plate capacitor having capacitance C in

options.text: the region between source and the plates
options.html: the region between source and the plates

questions.explanation: In the region between the plates no conducting current flows, hence Ampere's circuital law {tex}\oint\overrightarrow B.\overrightarrow {dl}=\mu_oI{/tex} does'nt hold correct.

Maxwell realized that the changing electric flux must be associated with a magnetic field. For example, for a parallel-plate capacitor: {tex}E = \frac{V}{d},\;V = \frac{Q}{C}{/tex} and {tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\right)_d}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}{/tex} Now, {tex}{\phi _E} = EA=\frac Q{\varepsilon_o} {/tex} and displacement current, i d = {tex}\frac {dQ}{dt}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{i_d} = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of these mechanisms can be used to produce electromagnetic waves?
questions.question_html: Which of these mechanisms can be used to produce electromagnetic waves?

options.text: point charge oscillating in simple harmonic motion
options.html: point charge oscillating in simple harmonic motion

options.text: point charge travelling in a straight line with constant velocity
options.html: point charge travelling in a straight line with constant velocity

questions.correct_option_text: point charge oscillating in simple harmonic motion
questions.explanation: This charge produces an oscillating electric field in its neighbourhood. This field in turn, produces an oscillating magnetic field in its neighbourhood. The process continues as both the fields acts as source of each other. Hence, an em wave originates from the oscillating charge. Note: The frequency of em wave is equal to frequency of oscillating charge.

questions.question_text: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in +ve z direction is described by
questions.question_html: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in +ve z direction is described by
questions.explanation: In em-wave, both electric as well as magnetic fields, are perpendicular to direction of propagation of wave. Since wave is propagating along x-axis, Electric field should be perpendicular to X-axis. It is given by {tex}{{\rm{E}}_{\rm{x}}} = {\rm{ }}{{\rm{E}}_0}{\rm{sin }}\left( {{\rm{kz}}-\omega {\rm{t}}} \right){/tex} , where k is propogation constant, and {tex}\omega{/tex} is angluar velocity.

questions.question_text: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in +ve z-direction is described by
questions.question_html: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in +ve z-direction is described by
questions.explanation: In an em-wave, both electric, as well as magnetic fields, are perpendicular to direction of propagation of wave. Hence B is given by {tex}{{\rm{B}}_{\rm{y}}} = {\rm{ }}{{\rm{B}}_0}{\rm{sin }}\left( {{\rm{ kz }}-{\rm{ }}\omega {\rm{t }}} \right){/tex} , where k is propagation constant and {tex}\omega{/tex} is angular velocity.

questions.question_text: The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic plane wave is
questions.question_html: The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic plane wave is

options.text: parallel to both electric field vector and the magnetic field vector
options.html: parallel to both electric field vector and the magnetic field vector

options.text: only perpendicular to electric field vector
options.html: only perpendicular to electric field vector

options.text: perpendicular to both electric field vector and the magnetic field vector
options.html: perpendicular to both electric field vector and the magnetic field vector

options.text: only perpendicular to magnetic field vector
options.html: only perpendicular to magnetic field vector

questions.correct_option_text: perpendicular to both electric field vector and the magnetic field vector
questions.explanation: Direction of propagation of em wave is given by Pointing vector which is given by {tex}\overrightarrow E\times \overrightarrow B{/tex} . Hence, it is perpendicular to both the field vectors.

questions.question_text: Wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} and wave vector k are related by
questions.question_html: Wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} and wave vector k are related by
questions.explanation: Wave vector, k is the spatial angular frequency of the wave, describing how many oscillations it completes per unit of space, and related to the wavelength by the equation k = {tex}\frac{2\pi } {\lambda }{/tex}
Since both electric and magnetic fields oscillates perpendicular to direction of propagation of em-wave, it is transverse in nature.

questions.question_text: In plane electromagnetic wave propagation velocity v, wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex} and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} are related by
questions.question_html: In plane electromagnetic wave propagation velocity v, wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex} and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} are related by

questions.question_text: Velocity of plane electromagnetic waves in vacuum equals
questions.question_html: Velocity of plane electromagnetic waves in vacuum equals
questions.explanation: In fact, light (visible) is a part of em-spectrum. Hence its speed is equal to that of light and in vacuum it is given by {tex}1\over \sqrt {\mu_o\epsilon_o}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Magnitude of the electric and the magnetic fields in an plane electromagnetic wave are related by
questions.question_html: Magnitude of the electric and the magnetic fields in an plane electromagnetic wave are related by
questions.explanation: {tex}{{\rm{B}}_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{E_0}}{c}{/tex} , where c is speed of light

questions.question_text: The speed of plane electromagnetic waves is maximum in
questions.question_html: The speed of plane electromagnetic waves is maximum in
questions.explanation: {tex}v= {1\over \sqrt{\mu_o\ \mu_r\epsilon_o\epsilon_r}}={c\over \sqrt{\epsilon_r\mu_r}}{/tex} Since, {tex}\epsilon_r\mu_r>1{/tex} , Thus, c (speed of light in vacuum) > v (speed of light in any other medium) Hence it is maximum in vacuum.

questions.question_text: Radio waves for FM radio have a frequency about
questions.question_html: Radio waves for FM radio have a frequency about
questions.explanation: Frequency band of FM broadcast is 88 - 108 MHz.
